The Commuter’s Dilemma: Stress, Delays, and Productivity

William Batkin

Introduction: The Daily Transit Log

For this project, I wanted to explore the reality of my daily commute. I reused my observational logging form from Project 2 to track my transit experiences, but expanded the scope to measure not just logistical delays, but my personal stress levels (on a scale of 1 to 5) and how I chose to spend my time during the journey. This data was collected over 20 weekday trips from myself, my sister and friends providing a snapshot of how environmental factors and bus time reliability impact overall well being and productivity.

The Weekly Grind: Tracking Stress

The first question I wanted to answer was whether certain days of the week are inherently more stressful than others. Does the anticipation of Monday or the exhaustion of Friday dictate the commuter experience?

A line graph showing the average stress levels of commutes from Monday to Friday. The y-axis ranges from 1 (Relaxed) to 5 (High Stress). The data points are connected by a thick blue line.

Looking at the timeline above, we can track the flow of commute induced stress across the university week. (Surprising higher peak at friday was not what was expected. For me lots of work is due on Friday/Monday so might contribute to the stress factor.)

Time Lost or Time Gained?

Transit delays are an inevitable part of using public transport. However, a delay only becomes “wasted time” depending on how that time is used. I categorised my commute activities into ‘Productive/Working’ (like studying or reviewing STATS 220 notes) and ‘Leisure/Resting’ (like listening to music) to see how my habits shifted when the bus ran late.

A jitter plot comparing Activity Category (Leisure vs Productive) against Minutes Late. Productive activities are marked in green, while Leisure activities are marked in red. Dots are scattered to show individual commute times.

This visualization reveals an interesting pattern regarding my productivity. By mapping the severity of the delay against my activity category, we can see whether long delays force me into a productive study mode or simply result in zoning out. (As you can see there was not much productivity during the bus rides)

Weathering the Storm

Finally, I wanted to examine the environmental impact on my transit experience. Auckland weather is notoriously unpredictable, but does a rainy day automatically guarantee a highly stressful commute?

A horizontal bar chart ranking weather conditions from least to most stressful. Each bar displays the sample size (n) of trips that occurred in that weather. The y-axis shows the average stress score on a locked 1 to 5 scale. A bus icon is composited into the bottom right corner.

By grouping the data by weather condition and calculating the mean stress score, a clear hierarchy emerges. This final visualisation potentially highlighting that environmental factors play a role in the psychological toll of commuting, completely independent of the actual transit vehicle itself.
